title: Trust Me, I'm Wrong: LLMs Hallucinate with Certainty Despite Knowing the Answer
abstract: Defines and investigates CHOKE (Certain Hallucinations Overriding Known Evidence) - a distinct type of hallucination where a model consistently answers correctly but a trivial perturbation causes high-certainty hallucination. Large and instruction-tuned models also produce these. Existing uncertainty-based mitigation fails against them.
relevance: HIGHLY RELEVANT - Directly supports the hypothesis that some hallucinations are robust to changes in how questions are asked, and that models hallucinate with high confidence even when they possess the correct knowledge. This is the closest existing work to 'natural hallucinations'.

title: LLMs Know More Than They Show: On the Intrinsic Representation of LLM Hallucinations
authors: Hadas Orgad, Michael Toker, Zorik Gekhman, Roi Reichart, Idan Szpektor, Hadas Kotek, Yonatan Belinkov
abstract: Published at ICLR 2025. Shows LLMs encode more truthfulness information internally than they express. Error detectors fail to generalize across datasets. LLMs may encode the correct answer internally yet consistently generate incorrect ones. Truthfulness encoding is multifaceted, not universal.
relevance: HIGHLY RELEVANT - Demonstrates that LLMs can 'know' the correct answer but still hallucinate, supporting the hypothesis that some hallucinations are robust and difficult for models to self-correct even with internal knowledge.

title: Distinguishing Ignorance from Error in LLM Hallucinations
authors: Adi Simhi, Jonathan Herzig, Idan Szpektor, Yonatan Belinkov
abstract: Distinguishes HK- hallucinations (model lacks knowledge) from HK+ (model has knowledge but answers incorrectly). HK+ hallucinations are prevalent across models and datasets. Different models hallucinate on different examples, motivating model-specific hallucination datasets.
relevance: HIGHLY RELEVANT - The HK+ category directly maps to 'natural hallucinations' where models fail despite having the information. The finding that different models hallucinate on different examples is relevant to cross-model transfer analysis.

title: Hallucination, Monofacts, and Miscalibration: An Empirical Investigation
abstract: First empirical investigation of the relationship between hallucination rate, monofact rate (facts seen once in training), and model miscalibration. Establishes that hallucination rate is lower-bounded by the monofact rate minus miscalibration, following Good-Turing estimator theory.
relevance: HIGHLY RELEVANT - Provides a theoretical framework explaining why certain hallucinations are intrinsic and predictable based on training data frequency, directly relevant to why some hallucinations may be 'natural' and persistent.

title: Hallucinate or Memorize? The Two Sides of Probabilistic Learning in Large Language Models
abstract: Shows hallucination and memorization are two sides of the same probabilistic process, determined by knowledge density in pretraining corpus. Highly cited papers (more frequent in corpus) show lower hallucination rates. LLM accuracy depends on whether knowledge is generated or memorized.
relevance: HIGHLY RELEVANT - Directly supports the hypothesis that hallucinations used in training could be more easily remembered, and that training data frequency determines hallucination vs memorization.

title: Why Language Models Hallucinate
abstract: Argues hallucinations arise because training/evaluation reward guessing over acknowledging uncertainty. If incorrect statements cannot be distinguished from facts, hallucinations arise through natural statistical pressures. Models are optimized as test-takers, and guessing improves test performance.
relevance: HIGHLY RELEVANT - The concept of 'natural statistical pressures' causing hallucinations aligns directly with the 'natural hallucinations' hypothesis. Provides theoretical grounding for why certain hallucinations are systematic.

title: Hallucination is Inevitable: An Innate Limitation of Large Language Models
abstract: Formalizes that LLMs cannot learn all computable functions and will inevitably hallucinate when used as general problem solvers. Hallucination is a structural limitation, not just an engineering problem.
relevance: HIGHLY RELEVANT - Theoretical foundation showing hallucinations are inevitable and structural, supporting the idea that some hallucinations are 'natural' and cannot be eliminated.

title: LLMs Will Always Hallucinate, and We Need to Live With This
authors: Sourav Banerjee, Ayushi Agarwal, Saloni Singla
abstract: Introduces 'Structural Hallucinations' as an intrinsic property of LLMs. Uses Goedel's Incompleteness Theorem and computational undecidability to show every stage of the LLM pipeline has non-zero hallucination probability regardless of model sophistication.
relevance: RELEVANT - Supports the theoretical inevitability of certain hallucinations, providing a formal basis for 'natural hallucinations' that resist elimination.

title: TruthfulQA: Measuring How Models Mimic Human Falsehoods
authors: Stephanie Lin, Jacob Hilton, Owain Evans
abstract: 817-question benchmark where largest models were least truthful. Models generate false answers mimicking popular misconceptions (imitative falsehoods). Performance decreases with model size, suggesting false answers are learned from training distribution.
relevance: HIGHLY RELEVANT - Foundational work showing LLMs systematically reproduce human misconceptions. The concept of 'imitative falsehoods' is a precursor to 'natural hallucinations'. The inverse scaling finding suggests these errors transfer and persist across model generations.

title: Sources of Hallucination by Large Language Models on Inference Tasks
abstract: Identifies memorization of training data and corpus-based heuristics as two major sources of hallucination across LLaMA, GPT-3.5, and PaLM. Named entities serve as indices to access memorized data, causing false labeling regardless of context.
relevance: HIGHLY RELEVANT - Shows hallucinations arise from memorization patterns shared across model families (LLaMA, GPT, PaLM), directly supporting cross-model hallucination transfer hypothesis.

title: Zero-knowledge LLM Hallucination Detection and Mitigation through Fine-grained Cross-Model Consistency (Finch-Zk)
abstract: Uses cross-model consistency to detect hallucinations without external knowledge. Key finding: consistency can inadvertently reinforce errors rather than expose them, as shared biases across model families represent a significant challenge.
relevance: HIGHLY RELEVANT - Directly demonstrates that hallucinations can be shared across model families and that cross-model consistency may amplify shared errors, supporting the cross-model transfer hypothesis.

title: Don't Hallucinate, Abstain: Identifying LLM Knowledge Gaps via Multi-LLM Collaboration
abstract: Uses collaboration between multiple LLMs to identify knowledge gaps. Addresses the challenge that knowledge gaps may always persist given the evolving nature of knowledge.
relevance: RELEVANT - Multi-LLM collaboration approach reveals shared knowledge gaps, relevant to understanding cross-model hallucination patterns.

title: The Phenomenology of Hallucinations
abstract: Shows LLMs hallucinate not because they fail to detect uncertainty but because of failure to integrate uncertainty into output generation. Uncertain inputs occupy high-dimensional regions (2-3x intrinsic dimensionality of factual inputs) but this signal becomes functionally silent at the output layer.
relevance: HIGHLY RELEVANT - Explains the mechanism by which LLMs can detect that something is uncertain internally but still hallucinate, supporting why 'natural hallucinations' are robust even when information is present.

title: Banishing LLM Hallucinations Requires Rethinking Generalization
abstract: Challenges conventional wisdom that hallucinations balance creativity and factuality. Shows through experiments that traditional approaches fail to explain practical hallucinations. Neural networks trained for next-token prediction hallucinate when training loss exceeds a threshold.
relevance: RELEVANT - Provides theoretical connection between training dynamics and hallucination persistence, relevant to understanding why certain hallucinations are fundamental.

title: Confabulation: The Surprising Value of Large Language Model Hallucinations
abstract: Explores confabulation as a narrative-driven tendency to fill in missing information with self-consistent stories bearing close verisimilitude with reality. Argues some hallucinations have surprising value.
relevance: RELEVANT - The concept of confabulation as self-consistent gap-filling relates to why natural hallucinations may be coherent and difficult to detect.

title: Can LLMs Detect Their Confabulations? Estimating Reliability in Uncertainty-Aware Language Models
abstract: Investigates whether LLMs can detect their own confabulations. Confabulations are fluent but ungrounded generations differing from truth subtly, making them difficult to detect.
relevance: RELEVANT - Addresses whether LLMs can self-detect hallucinations, relevant to the hypothesis that natural hallucinations are difficult for models to recognize.

title: Mitigating LLM Hallucination via Behaviorally Calibrated Reinforcement Learning
abstract: Hallucination is a predictable statistical consequence of training objectives. Standard RLHF inadvertently incentivizes models as test-takers rather than honest communicators, encouraging guessing when correctness probability exceeds zero. Proposes behavioral calibration to incentivize abstention.
relevance: RELEVANT - Shows hallucination arises from systematic training incentives, supporting the idea that certain hallucinations are natural consequences of the training process.

title: Chain-of-Verification Reduces Hallucination in Large Language Models
abstract: Model drafts initial response, plans verification questions, answers them independently, then generates a final verified response. Decreases hallucinations across various tasks.
relevance: RELEVANT - Tests whether providing additional verification information helps models correct hallucinations, relevant to the robustness aspect of the natural hallucinations hypothesis.

title: A Survey on Hallucination in Large Language Models: Principles, Taxonomy, Challenges, and Open Questions
authors: Lei Huang, Weijiang Yu et al.
abstract: Comprehensive survey providing taxonomy of hallucination types, contributing factors, detection methods, and mitigation strategies. Covers principles, challenges, and open questions.
relevance: RELEVANT - Major survey providing the taxonomic framework for understanding different hallucination types, useful background for positioning natural hallucinations research.

title: Large Language Models Hallucination: A Comprehensive Survey
abstract: Comprehensive survey covering causes, detection, and mitigation across the entire LLM development lifecycle. Taxonomy of detection techniques: retrieval-, uncertainty-, embedding-, learning-, and self-consistency-based.
relevance: RELEVANT - Comprehensive reference survey covering the full landscape of hallucination research.

title: Hallucination Detection and Mitigation in Large Language Models
abstract: Recent survey on hallucination detection and mitigation approaches in LLMs.
relevance: RELEVANT - Up-to-date survey on detection methods.

title: A Geometric Taxonomy of Hallucinations in LLMs
abstract: Proposes three types of hallucination with distinct geometric signatures: unfaithfulness, confabulation, and factual error. LLM-generated hallucinations show domain-local detection (AUROC 0.76-0.99 within domain, 0.50 across domains). Human-crafted confabulations achieve 0.96 global AUROC.
relevance: RELEVANT - The geometric distinction between hallucination types may help characterize natural hallucinations and explains why cross-domain detection fails.

title: Sycophancy in Large Language Models: Causes and Mitigations
abstract: Technical survey of sycophancy in LLMs. RLHF exacerbates sycophantic tendencies. Examines relationship between sycophancy and hallucination - models generate responses evaluators approve of regardless of accuracy.
relevance: RELEVANT - Sycophancy relates to why LLMs may be resistant to correcting hallucinations when presented with counter-evidence, connecting to the robustness aspect of natural hallucinations.

title: The Curious Case of Factual (Mis)Alignment between LLMs' Short- and Long-Form Answers
abstract: Examines 16 LLMs across 600 queries showing systematic misalignment between answers in short vs long queries. Models give different factual answers depending on query complexity.
relevance: RELEVANT - Shows hallucinations vary systematically with query format, relevant to testing robustness of hallucinations across question formulations.

title: The Earth is Flat? Unveiling Factual Errors in Large Language Models
abstract: Systematic investigation of factual errors in LLMs, unveiling patterns of when and how models produce incorrect factual claims.
relevance: RELEVANT - Systematic characterization of factual error patterns, relevant to identifying which errors may qualify as 'natural hallucinations'.

title: Why and How LLMs Hallucinate: Connecting the Dots with Subsequence Associations
abstract: Proposes LLMs rely on non-contiguous token subsequences which lead to hallucinations when representing incomplete or incorrect context.
relevance: RELEVANT - Mechanistic explanation for how hallucinations arise from token association patterns, relevant to understanding which hallucinations are systematic.

title: HalluLens: LLM Hallucination Benchmark
abstract: Comprehensive hallucination benchmark with extrinsic and intrinsic evaluation tasks, clear taxonomy, and dynamic test set generation to prevent data leakage.
relevance: MODERATELY RELEVANT - Benchmark for evaluating hallucinations, useful for testing natural hallucination hypotheses.

title: HaluEval: A Large-Scale Hallucination Evaluation Benchmark for Large Language Models
abstract: Large-scale benchmark for evaluating hallucination in LLMs across multiple tasks.
relevance: MODERATELY RELEVANT - Evaluation benchmark that could be used to test natural hallucination hypotheses.

title: Halluverse-M3: A Multitask Multilingual Benchmark for Hallucination in LLMs
abstract: Multitask multilingual benchmark for evaluating hallucination across languages and tasks.
relevance: MODERATELY RELEVANT - Cross-lingual hallucination evaluation, could reveal persistent hallucinations across languages.

title: When Models Lie, We Learn: Multilingual Span-Level Hallucination Detection with PsiloQA
abstract: Demonstrates cross-lingual generalization in hallucination detection, supporting robust knowledge transfer across benchmarks.
relevance: MODERATELY RELEVANT - Cross-lingual transfer of hallucination patterns relates to generalizability of hallucination detection.

title: ReFACT: A Benchmark for Scientific Confabulation Detection with Positional Error Annotations
abstract: Benchmark for detecting scientific confabulations with systematically embedded domain-specific errors in long-form scientific answers.
relevance: MODERATELY RELEVANT - Scientific confabulation benchmark relevant to studying persistent factual errors in specialized domains.

title: Mitigating Hallucination in Large Language Models: An Application-Oriented Survey on RAG, Reasoning, and Agentic Systems
abstract: Application-oriented survey analyzing how RAG, reasoning enhancement, and Agentic Systems mitigate hallucinations. Distinguishes knowledge-based and logic-based hallucinations.
relevance: MODERATELY RELEVANT - Survey of mitigation approaches, useful for understanding what works and what fails against robust hallucinations.

title: Measuring the Impact of Lexical Training Data Coverage on Hallucination Detection in Large Language Models
abstract: Studies how training data coverage affects hallucination detection. LLMs underperform on long-tail knowledge with accuracy dropping for rare ground-truth entities.
relevance: MODERATELY RELEVANT - Training data frequency effects on hallucination relate to the memorization aspect of the hypothesis.

title: LLM-based Agents Suffer from Hallucinations: A Survey
abstract: Survey of hallucination in LLM-based agents, covering taxonomy, methods, and directions.
relevance: MODERATELY RELEVANT - Extends hallucination analysis to agent settings.

title: Hallucination of Multimodal Large Language Models: A Survey
abstract: Survey of hallucination in multimodal LLMs covering evaluation benchmarks, metrics, and strategies.
relevance: MODERATELY RELEVANT - Multimodal perspective on hallucination.

title: A Comprehensive Survey of Hallucination Mitigation Techniques in Large Language Models
abstract: Comprehensive survey of hallucination mitigation techniques.
relevance: MODERATELY RELEVANT - Background survey on mitigation approaches.

title: Shaking to Reveal: Perturbation-Based Detection of LLM Hallucinations
abstract: Uses perturbation-based approaches to detect hallucinations by testing response stability under input variations.
relevance: RELEVANT - Perturbation-based detection directly tests whether hallucinations are robust to input changes, a core aspect of the natural hallucinations hypothesis.

title: Counterfactual Probing for Hallucination Detection and Mitigation in Large Language Models
abstract: Uses counterfactual probing to detect and mitigate hallucinations.
relevance: MODERATELY RELEVANT - Counterfactual approach relates to testing robustness of hallucinations.

title: Can LLMs Detect Their Own Hallucinations?
abstract: Investigates self-detection capabilities of LLMs for their own hallucinations.
relevance: RELEVANT - Directly tests whether LLMs can recognize their own hallucinations, a key aspect of the natural hallucinations hypothesis.

title: From Hallucinations to Facts: Enhancing Language Models with Curated Knowledge Graphs
abstract: Uses curated knowledge graphs to reduce hallucinations in LLMs.
relevance: MODERATELY RELEVANT - Tests whether providing structured knowledge eliminates hallucinations.

title: Factuality of Large Language Models in the Year 2024
abstract: Comprehensive assessment of LLM factuality as of 2024.
relevance: MODERATELY RELEVANT - Baseline for understanding current factuality levels across models.

title: DHI: Leveraging Diverse Hallucination Induction for Enhanced Contrastive Factuality Control
abstract: Uses diverse hallucination induction for contrastive factuality control in LLMs.
relevance: MODERATELY RELEVANT - Hallucination induction methods relevant to studying natural hallucination properties.
